label: Case Studies
heroTitle: Study full interview prompts without leaving the site.
heroDescription: Each case study explains the goal, main flow, useful components, trade-offs, and interview talking points in simple language.
cardIntro: Open any case study for a full breakdown with many examples, common mistakes, and practical interview guidance.

items.title: Design a URL Shortener
items.summary: Build a service that turns long links into short links and redirects users quickly.
quickPoints: Main writes: create short links.
Main reads: redirect short codes to original URLs.
This is read-heavy and latency-sensitive.
items.whatItMeans: You need a service that accepts a long URL, generates a short code, stores the mapping, and later redirects users when they visit the short link.
items.whyItMatters: This problem tests ID generation, read scaling, caching, and how you think about a very common but simple service.
items.simpleExample: A user submits a long shopping link and gets a short code like abc123. When someone visits that code, the service finds the original URL and redirects them.
realWorldExamples: Marketing teams use short links in campaigns.
Social apps use compact URLs in posts and messages.
Analytics products track click counts and link performance.
items.interviewTip: Start with the main flow: shorten, store, redirect. Only after that add analytics, custom aliases, and abuse control.
tradeOffs: Random IDs are easy to spread, but need collision handling.
Deterministic IDs are simpler to reason about, but may be predictable.
Caching redirects improves speed, but stale data can appear if links change or expire.
commonMistakes: Spending too much time on rare features before the redirect flow is clear.
Ignoring abuse, spam, or malicious URL control.
Forgetting that the redirect path is the hottest path.
abuseFraudNotes: Short-link products need malware, spam, and phishing controls as much as raw redirect scale.
Use reputation checks, suspicious-domain review, and per-account creation limits for abuse-heavy paths.
Analytics and abuse scoring can be asynchronous so the redirect path stays fast.
tenantIsolationNotes: If this becomes a B2B link platform, large marketing tenants may need separate quotas and analytics partitions.
Per-tenant rate limits help one customer campaign avoid degrading everyone else.

sections.title: Main architecture
paragraphs: A simple version has an API service, a database for short-code mappings, and a cache for popular redirects.
The create flow writes a new mapping. The read flow looks in cache first and then falls back to the database.

sections.title: Good follow-up points
paragraphs: After the main system is clear, talk about expiration, custom aliases, click analytics, and spam detection.
bullets: Analytics can be async through a queue.

items.title: Design a Chat System
items.summary: Build one-to-one or group messaging with real-time delivery, durable storage, and offline sync.
quickPoints: Messages should arrive quickly.
Users may go offline and come back later.
Ordering and fan-out matter.
items.whatItMeans: You need a system that lets users send messages, receive them fast, and view message history across devices.
items.whyItMatters: This problem tests real-time delivery, persistent connections, message storage, and scaling for fan-out.
items.simpleExample: One user sends hello. The other user sees it quickly if online, or later after reconnecting if offline.
realWorldExamples: Team chat tools need message history and search.
Consumer chat apps need group chats, media, and push notifications.
Support chat tools need message delivery tracking and agent routing.
items.interviewTip: Clarify if you need one-to-one chat, group chat, media, search, and message ordering before overdesigning.
tradeOffs: WebSockets give real-time behavior, but long-lived connections are expensive.
Strict ordering is useful, but harder at large scale.
Storing every event is powerful, but increases storage needs.
commonMistakes: Ignoring offline users and reconnect logic.
Forgetting push notifications for inactive clients.
Saying messages are always delivered exactly once without explaining how.

migrationPlaybook.title: Message-store evolution
migrationPlaybook.intro: Large chat systems usually outgrow their first message layout, so it helps to explain how history or conversation partitions can move safely.

phases.title: Version message records
phases.description: Add new schema or storage layout without breaking old readers.

phases.title: Backfill or replay carefully
phases.description: Move history in batches while keeping live delivery separate from migration load.

phases.title: Cut reads over by conversation or region
phases.description: Shift traffic gradually and verify ordering and sync behavior.

risks: Backfill load hurting live delivery latency
migrationPlaybook.closing: Messaging migrations should protect order and sync guarantees before they protect elegance.

sections.title: Core flow
paragraphs: Clients keep a real-time connection to chat gateways. A message is accepted, stored, and then delivered to online receivers or queued for later delivery.

sections.title: Important interview questions
paragraphs: Ask whether message order must be exact, whether group size can be large, and whether attachments or read receipts are needed.
bullets: How much history must be stored?

sections.title: Ordering and sync reality
paragraphs: Senior answers should say whether ordering is per conversation, how reconnect recovery works, and what happens when delayed or duplicate events arrive.
bullets: Use sequence numbers or version checks.
Treat duplicate delivery as normal, not rare.

items.title: Design a News Feed
items.summary: Build a system that shows users a feed of posts from people they follow, often with ranking and freshness trade-offs.
quickPoints: Posts must be written somewhere and shown to many readers.
Feed generation can happen on write or on read.
Popularity skew creates hot spots.
items.whatItMeans: A feed system gathers posts, decides which ones belong in a user's feed, and returns them in the right order.
items.whyItMatters: This problem tests fan-out, caching, ranking, and heavy read patterns.
items.simpleExample: If you follow ten people, your feed combines their new posts and sorts them so the app can show the most relevant ones first.
items.interviewTip: Explain the simple version first. Start with chronological feed before adding ranking and personalization.
tradeOffs: Fan-out on write makes reads fast, but writes become expensive.
Fan-out on read makes writes easy, but reads become slower.
Heavy ranking improves relevance, but adds compute cost and delay.
commonMistakes: Jumping straight into machine learning instead of basic feed generation.
Ignoring celebrity users with millions of followers.
Forgetting cache invalidation for fast-changing feeds.

sections.title: Useful baseline design
paragraphs: A baseline design includes post service, follow graph storage, feed generation logic, and a cache for fast reads.
bullets: Serve feed from cache when possible.

items.title: Design a Rate Limiter
items.summary: Build a system that limits how often a user or client can make requests in a time window.
quickPoints: Rate limiting protects systems and fair usage.
Checks must be very fast.
Distributed counters are often the tricky part.
items.whatItMeans: A rate limiter tracks request counts and decides whether the next request should be allowed or blocked.
items.whyItMatters: This problem tests fast lookups, distributed state, and fairness under heavy traffic.
items.simpleExample: A public API may allow 100 requests per minute per API key and reject extra requests until the window resets.
realWorldExamples: API gateways limiting traffic per client.
Messaging systems limiting spam from users.
items.interviewTip: Say where the limiter sits, how the counter is stored, and how exact the algorithm needs to be.
tradeOffs: Token bucket supports bursts, but needs token refill logic.
Sliding window is precise, but costs more to track.
Central storage is simple, but can become a bottleneck.
commonMistakes: Ignoring distributed deployments with many gateway servers.
Treating every endpoint the same even when abuse patterns differ.
Using a slow database lookup on every request.
tenantIsolationNotes: Large tenants or API plans may need separate quotas from free-tier traffic.
Global products often need different limits for auth, search, and write-heavy APIs.
Fairness matters as much as raw protection.
abuseFraudNotes: Rate limiting should separate abuse from real fraud, because fraud may stay under simple volume thresholds.
High-risk actions may need account reputation or step-up checks, not only counters.
Blocklists and behavioral signals often work together with rate limits.

sections.title: Good architecture shape
paragraphs: A common pattern is to place the limiter near the gateway and use a fast in-memory store such as Redis for counters.
bullets: Return clear error message if blocked.

items.title: Design YouTube
items.summary: Build a video platform with uploads, processing, playback, metadata, and global delivery.
quickPoints: Uploads are heavy writes.
Playback is a global read-heavy workload.
Video processing and CDN delivery are key.
items.whatItMeans: You need a platform where creators upload videos and viewers can stream them smoothly from many locations.
items.whyItMatters: This problem combines object storage, transcoding, CDN use, metadata indexing, and recommendation-friendly scaling.
items.simpleExample: A creator uploads one video once, but millions of viewers may stream different versions of it later on many devices.
items.interviewTip: Split the system into upload path and playback path. That keeps the answer clean.
tradeOffs: Precompute many video formats for smooth playback, but pay more storage and processing cost.
On-demand generation saves upfront work, but can delay playback.
Global CDN delivery is fast, but invalidation and regional rules add complexity.
commonMistakes: Treating video files like small images.
Ignoring transcoding delay and worker scaling.
Forgetting metadata search, comments, or recommendation side data.

sections.title: Upload path
paragraphs: The upload path usually goes from client to upload service to object storage, then a queue triggers transcoding workers.

sections.title: Playback path
paragraphs: The playback path usually reads metadata from a service and serves video segments through a CDN so viewers see low latency.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design YouTube and separate upload from playback clearly.
checkpoints: Start with upload path and processing.
Then explain playback and CDN delivery.
Mention metadata, search, and analytics only after the core flow.

mockConversation.speaker: Interviewer
mockConversation.text: Why are you separating upload and playback?

mockConversation.speaker: Candidate
mockConversation.text: Because upload is a write-heavy processing path, while playback is a global read-heavy path with very different bottlenecks.

failureScenarios.title: Transcoding backlog grows quickly
failureScenarios.issue: Uploads succeed, but processed formats take too long to appear.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Check queue backlog, worker saturation, job priority, and whether lower-priority formats can be delayed to protect core playback readiness.

seniorConsiderations: Multi-region playback needs clear content placement and cache warm-up strategy.
Backpressure is important when uploads and processing spikes happen together.
Observability should separate upload failure, processing lag, and playback startup issues.
multiRegionNotes: Store and process content close to ingestion when possible, then replicate popular media to regions with demand.
Playback traffic should be served from the edge whenever possible.
observabilityNotes: Track upload success rate, transcoding queue lag, manifest latency, and playback startup time.
Separate metadata service failures from actual media delivery failures.
securityPrivacyNotes: Protect upload authorization and signed media access carefully.
Moderation, abuse review, and privacy rules can affect storage and delivery workflows.

items.title: Design Uber
items.summary: Build a system that tracks drivers, matches riders, manages trips, and updates locations in real time.
quickPoints: Location updates arrive constantly.
Matching should be fast and local.
Trip state must stay correct even during failures.
items.whatItMeans: You need a ride platform where riders request trips, nearby drivers receive matches, and both sides see live updates.
items.whyItMatters: This problem combines real-time location, geospatial queries, dispatch logic, pricing, and fault tolerance.
items.simpleExample: A rider requests a trip, the system finds nearby drivers, selects a good candidate, and keeps both sides updated as the trip moves forward.
items.interviewTip: Separate location tracking, matching, and trip management. That avoids one giant fuzzy design.
tradeOffs: Frequent location updates improve accuracy, but increase write load.
Centralized matching is simple, but regional dispatch may be faster.
Aggressive surge pricing responds quickly, but can hurt user trust.
commonMistakes: Ignoring how often drivers send updates.
Mixing trip state logic with raw location ingestion.
Forgetting idempotency when retries happen during trip updates.

sections.title: Key services
paragraphs: A practical design has location ingestion, geospatial index or store, matching service, trip service, pricing service, and notification path.
bullets: Push updates to rider and driver.

items.title: Design Dropbox
items.summary: Build a file storage system that uploads files, syncs them across devices, and keeps metadata and versions organized.
quickPoints: Large files should be split into chunks.
Metadata and file content usually scale differently.
Conflict handling matters when multiple devices edit.
items.whatItMeans: You need a service where users upload files, store them safely, and access the same versions from many devices.
items.whyItMatters: This problem tests chunking, metadata design, syncing, conflict resolution, and background processing.
items.simpleExample: A user edits a document on a laptop and then sees the updated version on a phone after sync finishes.
realWorldExamples: Photo backup apps with cross-device sync.
items.interviewTip: Split the problem into file content storage, metadata storage, and sync orchestration.
tradeOffs: Chunk-level sync saves bandwidth, but increases implementation complexity.
Frequent sync feels fast, but costs more network and battery.
Automatic conflict merging is convenient, but risky for binary files.
commonMistakes: Treating metadata and blobs as if they belong in the same storage design.
Ignoring version history.
Skipping conflict resolution for offline edits.

migrationPlaybook.title: Storage and metadata migration
migrationPlaybook.intro: File systems often need metadata or chunk-layout changes long after the product launches.

phases.title: Version metadata first
phases.description: Keep enough metadata to know which chunk or version layout each file uses.

phases.title: Move low-risk files gradually
phases.description: Backfill old files in batches while protecting the live sync path.

phases.title: Cut sync and download traffic over
phases.description: Move readers and sync workers only after integrity and permission checks pass.

migrationPlaybook.closing: Storage migrations sound senior when you explain how the system changes without breaking existing files.

securityPrivacyNotes: Retention and deletion rules should cover file content, metadata, thumbnails, caches, and indexes together.
Sensitive shared files may need stronger audit logging and export controls.

sections.title: A simple system split
paragraphs: One useful pattern is to store file chunks in object storage and store file metadata, version pointers, and permissions in a structured metadata service.

sections.title: Where conflicts appear
paragraphs: Conflicts happen when users edit the same file from different devices before sync completes. Text files may merge more easily than binary files.

items.title: Design Netflix
items.summary: Build a video streaming system that delivers movies and shows smoothly to a huge global audience.
quickPoints: Playback is the hottest path.
Catalog, recommendations, and streaming are different subsystems.
CDN and adaptive streaming are central.
items.whatItMeans: You need a platform where users discover content, start playback quickly, and continue streaming reliably as network conditions change.
items.whyItMatters: This problem tests large-scale media delivery, metadata services, recommendation-friendly architecture, and global read performance.
items.simpleExample: A user opens the app, sees a catalog, starts a movie in seconds, and the stream adjusts quality as the network changes.
items.interviewTip: Split discovery, playback, and analytics into separate conversations so the answer stays clean.
tradeOffs: Pre-encoded formats improve startup speed, but increase storage cost.
Global CDN delivery lowers latency, but invalidation and regional rules get harder.
Heavy recommendations improve engagement, but add another large compute system.
commonMistakes: Mixing recommendation design with the playback path too early.
Ignoring adaptive bitrate streaming.
Treating large media files like simple database rows.

stepFlow.title: Show the catalog
stepFlow.description: Metadata and recommendation services build the homepage and title details.

stepFlow.title: Start playback
stepFlow.description: Playback service checks entitlement and returns the stream manifest.

stepFlow.title: Stream from the edge
stepFlow.description: The client fetches video segments from a CDN close to the user.

stepFlow.title: Track and improve
stepFlow.description: Watch events flow asynchronously into analytics and recommendation systems.

diagramBlocks.title: Netflix architecture view
diagramBlocks.footer: Keep the playback path lean. Discovery and recommendation can be discussed as separate supporting systems.

whatInterviewerExpects: A clean split between metadata, playback, and analytics.
Awareness that CDN and adaptive streaming are essential.
Clear explanation of why global reads dominate the design.
followUpQuestions: How would you support downloads for offline viewing?
Where would recommendations fit into this architecture?

sections.title: Main system split
paragraphs: A strong answer separates metadata services from the actual media delivery path. The playback path should stay simple and fast.
bullets: Playback service for entitlement and manifests.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design Netflix and explain catalog, playback, and analytics without mixing them.
checkpoints: Explain manifest and segment delivery clearly.

mockConversation.speaker: Interviewer
mockConversation.text: What would you optimize first for user experience?

mockConversation.speaker: Candidate
mockConversation.text: Playback startup time and stream stability, because users notice those immediately. Recommendation quality matters too, but it is not the hottest part of the media path.

answerQualityLevels.summary: Separates metadata and playback and mentions CDN.
Explains adaptive bitrate, analytics, and global delivery with strong prioritization.
Shows production-level thinking across playback, observability, and global rollout.
traits: Separates data plane and support systems.
Mentions startup time, rebuffering, and edge health.
Explains what to monitor and optimize first.

failureScenarios.title: Playback startup slows globally
failureScenarios.issue: Users can browse titles, but many streams start slowly in multiple regions.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Check manifest service latency, CDN edge health, origin miss rate, and segment generation path before blaming the whole platform.

seniorConsiderations: The playback path should be protected from recommendation and analytics overload.
Global failover and content placement decisions are major senior-level concerns here.
Observability should include startup delay, rebuffer rate, and edge-cache performance.
multiRegionNotes: Different regions may need different cache strategies and replication timing based on demand.
Some metadata systems may stay global while playback delivery stays edge-heavy and regional.
observabilityNotes: Track startup time, rebuffering, CDN hit rate, manifest latency, and error rate by region.
Use playback quality metrics, not only request counts, to judge user experience.

migrationPlaybook.title: Playback rollout playbook
migrationPlaybook.intro: Streaming systems often release manifest changes, player behavior changes, or encoding changes gradually because the blast radius is large.

phases.title: Canary one region or device group
phases.description: Start with a small slice so playback regressions are visible early.

phases.title: Compare startup and rebuffer metrics
phases.description: Use user-experience metrics, not only server health, to judge the new path.

phases.title: Expand slowly and keep rollback ready
phases.description: Grow traffic only while edge and origin behavior stay healthy.

risks: Healthy server metrics masking bad user playback
Encoding or manifest mismatches on specific device classes
migrationPlaybook.closing: The senior move is protecting playback quality while the platform evolves.

costModel.title: Streaming cost hotspots
costModel.intro: Global video systems are often dominated by storage, transcoding, and bandwidth cost.
optimizationLevers: Choose the most valuable encodings first
Use CDN and cache placement intelligently
whenToSpendMore: Startup-time improvements on the hottest markets
costModel.closing: Senior streaming answers explain what the next playback improvement costs.

items.title: Design WhatsApp
items.summary: Build a large global messaging platform with fast delivery, offline sync, media sharing, and strong reliability.
quickPoints: Realtime messaging is central.
Offline delivery matters as much as online delivery.
Group fan-out and media storage add major scale pressure.
items.whatItMeans: You need a system that sends messages quickly between users and groups, keeps message history, and supports media attachments across devices.
items.whyItMatters: This problem combines persistent connections, fan-out, storage, notification delivery, and global scale.
items.simpleExample: A user sends a text and a photo in one city. The receiver should get them quickly, even if they reconnect later from another device.
items.interviewTip: Separate text delivery, media delivery, and presence. Do not put every messaging feature into one service box.
tradeOffs: Realtime connections feel fast, but open connections are expensive.
Large groups increase fan-out cost.
End-to-end security can improve privacy, but may reduce server-side feature options.
commonMistakes: Ignoring offline devices and message sync.
Not explaining group fan-out.
Forgetting media upload and download flow.

stepFlow.title: User sends message
stepFlow.description: Client sends the message to a chat gateway over a persistent connection.

stepFlow.title: Store and route
stepFlow.description: Message service writes the message and routes it to online or offline receivers.

stepFlow.title: Deliver or queue
stepFlow.description: Online users receive it instantly. Offline users get it after reconnect or push notification.

stepFlow.title: Sync other devices
stepFlow.description: The system updates message state across the sender's and receiver's devices.

diagramBlocks.title: WhatsApp architecture view
diagramBlocks.footer: Keep the message path separate from media upload and download. That makes the design easier to explain.

whatInterviewerExpects: Clear handling of online and offline users.
A plan for group messaging and message ordering.
Awareness that media should use a different path from text.
followUpQuestions: How do you scale group chats with many members?
What changes if read receipts are required?
How would you sync history across devices?

sections.title: Good interview structure
paragraphs: Start with one-to-one text messages, then expand to groups, media, and multi-device sync only after the basic path is solid.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design WhatsApp and explain how online and offline users are both handled well.

failureScenarios.title: Large reconnect wave after outage
failureScenarios.issue: Millions of clients reconnect at once and message delivery slows.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Use reconnect backoff, gateway scaling, queue smoothing, and priority on critical delivery paths.

seniorConsiderations: Presence, delivery guarantees, and group fan-out must be reasoned about separately.
Privacy and encryption can change what the server is allowed to do.
Cross-region messaging needs careful ownership and latency planning.
multiRegionNotes: Keep message routing as local as possible, but ensure user history and device sync still work across regions.
Global products may need region-aware presence and delivery services.
securityPrivacyNotes: End-to-end encryption changes storage, search, and moderation options.
Metadata privacy may still matter even when content is protected.

sections.title: Good interview structure
paragraphs: Start with one-to-one text messages, then expand to groups, media, and multi-device sync only after the basic path is solid.

sections.title: Ordering and device sync
paragraphs: Senior answers should explain whether order is per conversation, how duplicate delivery is handled, and how the system keeps multiple devices consistent after reconnect.
bullets: Handle late or duplicate events safely.
Keep multi-device sync and media delivery as separate concerns.

items.title: Design Google Drive
items.summary: Build a cloud storage and collaboration system with file upload, versioning, sharing, sync, and large-scale metadata handling.
quickPoints: Files and metadata should be separated.
Sync and versioning are core features.
Collaboration adds consistency and conflict challenges.
items.whatItMeans: You need a system where users upload files, share them, sync changes across devices, and sometimes edit them collaboratively.
items.whyItMatters: This problem mixes storage, sync, permissions, metadata, and collaboration concerns in one product.
items.simpleExample: A user uploads a presentation, shares it with a teammate, and both users can see the latest version on laptop and phone.
items.interviewTip: Separate blob storage, metadata, and collaboration logic. That keeps the answer modular.
tradeOffs: Chunk-level sync saves bandwidth, but increases complexity.
Strong collaboration support improves teamwork, but raises consistency challenges.
Version history improves safety, but adds storage cost.
commonMistakes: Treating files and metadata as one storage problem.
Ignoring permission checks and sharing links.
Skipping version history and conflict handling.

stepFlow.title: Upload file
stepFlow.description: Client uploads file chunks to object storage and creates metadata records.

stepFlow.title: Sync metadata
stepFlow.description: Metadata service tracks ownership, versions, permissions, and file state.

stepFlow.title: Share and access
stepFlow.description: Other users access the file through permission checks and metadata lookup.

stepFlow.title: Update and version
stepFlow.description: New edits create new versions and trigger sync notifications to devices.

whatInterviewerExpects: A clear split between file content and metadata.
Awareness of versioning and sharing permissions.
A plan for sync across multiple devices.
followUpQuestions: How would you support collaborative document editing?
How do you handle a failed chunk upload?
What if two users edit offline and sync later?

migrationPlaybook.title: File-platform evolution
migrationPlaybook.intro: Drive-style systems often evolve metadata stores, sharing models, and sync pipelines independently.

phases.title: Version metadata and permissions
phases.description: Keep enough schema information so old and new sharing models can both be understood.

phases.title: Migrate background sync safely
phases.description: Move sync workers or indexes in waves so active collaboration stays healthy.

phases.title: Retire old file views and paths
phases.description: Only remove old metadata or sharing behavior after all major clients are proven on the new path.

risks: Deletion rules not matching across stores
migrationPlaybook.closing: Senior storage answers usually mention how metadata and permissions change safely over time.

sections.title: Metadata matters
paragraphs: Many file systems look simple until metadata queries grow. Search, sharing, file listings, and version tracking all depend on good metadata design.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design Google Drive and explain how metadata, blob storage, and sync differ.

failureScenarios.title: Conflicting offline edits appear after reconnect
failureScenarios.issue: Two users edit from different devices before sync completes.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Explain version IDs, conflict copies or merge logic, and how metadata records preserve recoverable history.

seniorConsiderations: Large-scale sync products live or die by metadata quality and conflict policies.
Collaboration features can require different consistency rules than passive file sync.
Operational recovery for failed chunk uploads should be part of the design.
multiRegionNotes: Blob storage can replicate differently from metadata services.
Regional placement decisions may depend on compliance and access patterns.
securityPrivacyNotes: Sharing links, permissions, and document visibility rules are central security concerns.
Sensitive files may require stronger auditing, encryption, and deletion workflows.

items.title: Design Instagram
items.summary: Build a photo and short-video social platform with uploads, feeds, likes, comments, follow graph, and notifications.
quickPoints: Media upload and feed reading are both important.
The follow graph drives feed and notification behavior.
Hot users create skew and fan-out challenges.
items.whatItMeans: You need a social media app where users post media, follow others, browse feeds, and interact with content in near real time.
items.whyItMatters: This problem combines media storage, feeds, graph relationships, engagement actions, and notification delivery.
items.simpleExample: A user uploads a photo, followers see it in the feed, some users like it, and the owner receives notifications.
items.interviewTip: Split the problem into media upload, feed generation, and engagement actions instead of mixing everything together.
tradeOffs: Fan-out on write speeds feed reads, but can be expensive for large accounts.
Rich media features improve engagement, but increase storage and CDN cost.
Realtime notifications feel good, but add extra event flow complexity.
commonMistakes: Ignoring the follow graph.
Treating media processing as a small detail.
Skipping comment, like, and notification fan-out.

stepFlow.title: Upload media
stepFlow.description: The client uploads media, which is processed and stored in object storage.

stepFlow.title: Create post metadata
stepFlow.description: Post service records owner, captions, tags, and media locations.

stepFlow.title: Update feeds
stepFlow.description: Feed systems fan out or compute updates for followers.

stepFlow.title: Send engagement updates
stepFlow.description: Likes, comments, and notifications move through async event paths.

whatInterviewerExpects: A clear split between media storage and feed generation.
Awareness of the follow graph and celebrity skew.
A plan for notifications and engagement events.

sections.title: Most important split
paragraphs: Keep upload and feed generation as separate conversations. One is write and media heavy. The other is read and ranking heavy.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design X or Twitter and explain the trade-off between fan-out on write and fan-out on read.

failureScenarios.title: A celebrity post overloads timeline generation
failureScenarios.issue: One write affects millions of timelines and queues back up.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Explain how to degrade gracefully, shift strategy for hot accounts, and protect reader latency.

seniorConsiderations: Celebrity skew and global event spikes are major production concerns.
Timeline freshness, ranking quality, and search freshness may need separate scaling paths.
Operational visibility into fan-out cost is crucial.
observabilityNotes: Track timeline generation lag, cache hit rate, fan-out queue depth, and search freshness lag.
Use separate metrics for hot-account events and normal traffic.

items.title: Design X or Twitter
items.summary: Build a social platform with short posts, timelines, follow graph, search, trends, and realtime updates.
quickPoints: Timelines are read-heavy.
Trending and search add separate systems.
Very popular accounts create extreme fan-out pressure.
items.whatItMeans: You need a platform where users post short messages, follow other users, and receive updated timelines quickly.
items.whyItMatters: This classic problem tests feed design, graph scale, hot-key handling, search, and event-driven updates.
items.simpleExample: A celebrity posts once, but millions of followers may need that post in their timelines almost immediately.
items.interviewTip: Talk about timelines first. Search, trends, and notifications can come after the core timeline path is clear.
tradeOffs: Fan-out on write is fast for readers, but hard for huge accounts.
Fan-out on read saves writes, but can slow heavy readers.
Realtime ranking and trends feel lively, but add more streaming infrastructure.
commonMistakes: Not addressing celebrity skew.
Mixing search and timeline architecture from the start.
Ignoring caching and timeline freshness.

stepFlow.title: Create post
stepFlow.description: The post service saves the post and emits an event.

stepFlow.title: Update timelines
stepFlow.description: Timeline services fan out or compute feeds for followers.

stepFlow.title: Serve readers quickly
stepFlow.description: Caches hold hot timeline data for heavy readers.

stepFlow.title: Power search and trends
stepFlow.description: Events also update search indexes and trend systems.

whatInterviewerExpects: A strong explanation of timeline generation.
Handling of celebrity skew.
Separation between timeline, search, and trends.

sections.title: Where the pressure appears
paragraphs: The biggest pressure often appears when one write must quickly affect huge numbers of readers.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design X or Twitter and explain how you would handle celebrity traffic.
checkpoints: Separate search and trends from timeline delivery.

failureScenarios.title: Celebrity event causes feed lag
failureScenarios.issue: One high-profile post creates huge fan-out pressure and timeline queues back up.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Discuss hot-account handling, partial fan-out strategies, stronger caching, and protecting reader latency during event spikes.

seniorConsiderations: Timeline freshness, search freshness, and trend freshness may scale very differently.
Hot-account behavior often needs special paths instead of one global strategy.
The system should degrade gracefully during major events, not simply fail.
multiRegionNotes: Global events can create synchronized spikes across many regions at once.
Regional caches and event pipelines may need different warm-up and failover strategies.
observabilityNotes: Track hot-account fan-out cost, timeline lag, cache hit rate, and trend-pipeline delay.
Watch user-visible freshness, not only internal queue depth.

items.title: Design Zoom
items.summary: Build a video meeting platform with live audio/video transport, meeting coordination, chat, recording, and participant management.
quickPoints: Realtime media quality is the core challenge.
Meeting state and signaling are different from media transport.
Recording and chat can be separate supporting paths.
items.whatItMeans: You need a system where users join live meetings, exchange audio and video in real time, and manage meeting controls and chat.
items.whyItMatters: This problem combines realtime transport, session coordination, state management, and large bandwidth handling.
items.simpleExample: Ten people join a video call. They expect low delay audio, stable video, mute controls, chat, and maybe cloud recording.
items.interviewTip: Separate meeting setup and participant state from the actual audio and video path.
tradeOffs: Lower latency improves conversation quality, but is harder over bad networks.
Server-side media routing improves manageability, but increases bandwidth cost.
Recording adds value, but creates a heavy storage pipeline.
commonMistakes: Treating media streaming like normal HTTP APIs.
Ignoring network quality adaptation.
Not separating signaling from media transport.

stepFlow.title: Create or join meeting
stepFlow.description: Meeting service authenticates users and returns meeting state.

stepFlow.title: Negotiate media path
stepFlow.description: Signaling sets up how audio and video will be exchanged.

stepFlow.title: Exchange live media
stepFlow.description: Participants send and receive audio and video with low delay.

stepFlow.title: Support extras
stepFlow.description: Chat, recording, participant controls, and analytics run beside the media path.

whatInterviewerExpects: A clear split between signaling and media transport.
Awareness of low latency and network variability.
A basic plan for many participants and optional recording.

sections.title: Keep the answer manageable
paragraphs: You do not need codec-level detail in most interviews. Focus on meeting coordination, low-latency media routing, and scale concerns.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design Zoom and explain signaling, media flow, and recording clearly.
checkpoints: Separate control plane from media plane.
Mention recording and chat as support paths.

failureScenarios.title: Meeting quality drops under participant growth
failureScenarios.issue: Audio and video degrade as the participant count rises.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Discuss media routing strategy, selective stream forwarding, quality adaptation, and participant-limit policies.

seniorConsiderations: Realtime media systems need strict focus on latency, not just correctness.
The control plane should stay useful even if media quality degrades temporarily.
Observability should include user-perceived quality, not only server health.
observabilityNotes: Track join latency, media packet loss, end-to-end delay, and reconnect rates.
Monitor meeting quality by geography and network type.

items.title: Design a Payment System
items.summary: Build a system that creates, authorizes, captures, and tracks payments safely with strong correctness and auditability.
quickPoints: Correctness matters more than flashy scale ideas.
Idempotency is essential.
Audit logs, retries, and state transitions must be clear.
items.whatItMeans: You need a payment platform that takes payment requests, communicates with processors, records state changes, and avoids double charging.
items.whyItMatters: This problem tests correctness, reliability, idempotency, and clear state modeling under failure.
items.simpleExample: A user clicks pay, the request times out, and the app retries. The system must not charge the card twice.
items.interviewTip: Keep the state machine clear: created, authorized, captured, failed, refunded, or cancelled depending on the flow.
tradeOffs: Strong correctness improves trust, but may add latency and operational checks.
Synchronous confirmation feels faster to users, but external processors may be slow.
Rich audit logging improves safety, but adds storage and privacy considerations.
commonMistakes: Ignoring idempotency.
Not modeling payment states clearly.
Treating payment retries like normal retries without side-effect protection.

stepFlow.title: Create payment request
stepFlow.description: Client sends a payment request with an idempotency key.

stepFlow.title: Validate and authorize
stepFlow.description: Payment service validates the request and sends authorization to an external processor.

stepFlow.title: Store final state
stepFlow.description: The system records success, failure, or pending state in a durable store.

stepFlow.title: Trigger follow-up actions
stepFlow.description: Order confirmation, ledger updates, and notifications happen after payment state is safe.

diagramBlocks.title: Payment architecture view
diagramBlocks.footer: Correctness, auditability, and safe retries matter more here than fancy scaling words.

whatInterviewerExpects: Strong emphasis on correctness.
A clear payment state machine.
A safe retry and idempotency strategy.
followUpQuestions: How do you handle a timeout after the processor may already have charged?
How do you reconcile internal and external payment records?
Would you separate authorization and capture?

relatedTopics.title: SLIs, SLOs, SLAs, and Error Budgets

sections.title: What matters most
paragraphs: This is not just another CRUD system. The hardest part is making sure the money state stays correct under retries, failures, and slow external dependencies.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design a payment system and explain how you avoid duplicate charges.
checkpoints: Use a clear payment state model.

mockConversation.speaker: Interviewer
mockConversation.text: Why are you spending so much time on state and idempotency?

mockConversation.speaker: Candidate
mockConversation.text: Because in payments the biggest failure is not only downtime. It is incorrect money movement, duplicate charges, or missing auditability.

failureScenarios.title: Processor timeout after authorization may have succeeded
failureScenarios.issue: The client retries, but the upstream provider may already have accepted the charge.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Use idempotency keys, store request state durably, and reconcile with the provider before creating a second charge path.

seniorConsiderations: Correctness, auditability, and reconciliation matter more than flashy scale vocabulary.
Multi-region payments may need careful regional ownership and ledger consistency rules.
Operational incident handling should be part of the design story.

sloPack.title: Payment reliability targets
sloPack.intro: Payments need both normal availability targets and correctness-sensitive indicators.
indicators: P95 latency on the checkout critical path
targets: Fast enough checkout response for user trust
Strict reconciliation targets for internal correctness
errorBudgetPolicies: Slow risky payment changes when correctness signals degrade
Prefer safe degradation over throughput during payment incidents
Use rollback or provider-routing changes quickly when processor quality drops
interviewMoves: Name one user-facing and one correctness-facing metric
Explain how release decisions change when the budget is under pressure
sloPack.closing: Payment systems sound senior when reliability includes money correctness, not only request success.

incidentRunbook.title: Payment incident flow
incidentRunbook.intro: Payment incidents are dangerous because wrong money movement can be worse than short downtime.

stages.title: Stop risky actions
stages.description: Pause unsafe retries, route away from a bad provider, or freeze a risky payment path if correctness is uncertain.

stages.title: Protect correctness
stages.description: Check idempotency, duplicate-charge risk, and reconciliation state before resuming normal flow.

stages.title: Recover and reconcile
stages.description: Bring the payment flow back, then run reconciliation and customer-remediation steps if needed.

immediateActions: Separate provider errors from internal state-machine errors
Use a manual or delayed review path when money state is uncertain
communicationRules: Be precise about money risk and user impact
Keep finance, support, and engineering aligned on status
recoveryChecks: Authorization and capture rates are healthy
postmortemActions: Tighten rollout, provider-failover, or idempotency safeguards
incidentRunbook.closing: Senior payment thinking is calm, correctness-first incident handling.

abuseFraudNotes: Separate card fraud, account takeover, and traffic abuse because they need different defenses.
High-risk payments may need step-up checks, holds, or manual review, not just rate limits.
Fraud systems should feed back into risk scoring and provider-routing decisions.
multiRegionNotes: Regional payment rules and processor integrations may differ by geography.
Cross-region failover must not create double-charge risk.
observabilityNotes: Track authorization success rate, retry rate, reconciliation mismatch rate, and duplicate-request detection.
Separate processor issues from internal state-machine bugs.
securityPrivacyNotes: Protect card and account data with strong segregation, encryption, and access auditing.
Tokenization and compliance boundaries should be part of the architecture discussion.

items.title: Design a Notification System
items.summary: Build a system that sends email, push, SMS, or in-app notifications at scale while respecting priority, preference, and delivery reliability.
quickPoints: Notifications are often asynchronous.
Different channels have different delivery rules.
User preferences and rate limits matter.
items.whatItMeans: You need a platform that receives events, decides which users should be notified, chooses channels, and sends messages reliably.
items.whyItMatters: This problem tests event-driven design, fan-out, template management, delivery pipelines, and overload protection.
items.simpleExample: A user gets an order shipped event. The system may send push first, then email, and may skip SMS if the user disabled it.
items.interviewTip: Separate event ingestion, preference lookup, message composition, and channel delivery.
tradeOffs: More channels improve reach, but increase delivery complexity.
Realtime delivery feels useful, but some notifications can be batched more cheaply.
Aggressive fan-out helps engagement, but can become spam without limits.
commonMistakes: Sending notifications without checking preferences.
Treating SMS, push, and email as if they behave the same way.
Ignoring deduplication and rate limits.

stepFlow.title: Receive event
stepFlow.description: The notification system receives an event from another service.

stepFlow.title: Decide who should get what
stepFlow.description: Preference and rules services decide channels, templates, and priority.

stepFlow.title: Queue delivery work
stepFlow.description: Messages move into separate channel-specific queues and workers.

stepFlow.title: Track delivery results
stepFlow.description: Delivery status, retries, and user interaction events are recorded.

whatInterviewerExpects: A clean event-driven design.
Respect for user preference and channel differences.
A plan for spikes, retries, and deduplication.
followUpQuestions: How would you avoid sending duplicate notifications?
What if a push provider is temporarily down?
How would you support high-priority notifications during overload?

sections.title: Where most designs go wrong
paragraphs: Many simple notification designs forget that preferences, limits, retries, and delivery providers are all different from one another.

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Design a notification system with push, email, and SMS.

failureScenarios.title: Push provider outage during traffic spike
failureScenarios.issue: Push delivery fails while a major product event triggers millions of notifications.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Use channel-specific queues, priority handling, provider failover or retry policy, and load shedding for low-priority notifications.

seniorConsiderations: Notification systems need strong abuse controls and product-priority rules.
Backpressure matters because fan-out can grow much faster than user-facing traffic.
Delivery observability should include both provider health and user-level outcomes.
observabilityNotes: Track queue depth by channel, provider error rate, retry rate, and user delivery success.
Separate template bugs from provider delivery failures.
securityPrivacyNotes: Notification content can leak sensitive data, so templates and channel rules should be privacy-aware.
Preference and opt-out records must be durable and respected across all delivery paths.
tenantIsolationNotes: Large tenants or premium tenants may need separate quotas and worker pools during spikes.
Priority rules should stop one tenant's campaign from blocking password resets or fraud alerts for others.
Per-tenant observability should show who is creating queue pressure.

sloPack.title: Notification reliability targets
sloPack.intro: Senior answers often separate urgent notifications from bulk engagement or marketing traffic.
targets: Fast delivery for urgent transactional messages
Stronger tolerance for delay on low-priority bulk traffic
errorBudgetPolicies: Protect urgent notifications first during overload
Shed or delay low-priority campaigns when the budget is under pressure
Pause risky provider changes when delivery health is poor
interviewMoves: Tie the target to user harm and product value
sloPack.closing: Notification systems sound senior when they distinguish urgent delivery from bulk traffic.
